Eddy Identification — MATLAB vs Python Comparison

eddy_identification_winding · Delaware Bay sample · 2020-04-03 09:00 UTC

Four implementations, one anticyclonic eddy

The bundled 2 km HF-radar sample 202004030900_hfr_usegc_2km_rtv_uwls_NDBC.nc run through the current GitHub MATLAB code, the older v1.0 MATLAB and Python code, and the new Python v2 port of the MATLAB upgrades. All find the same clockwise eddy just seaward of the Delaware Bay mouth — and the v2 port matches MATLAB to 7 decimal places. The full tracking pipeline was then run by both MATLAB and Python v2 on the 101-timestep data2 example: identical eddy counts, identical tracks.

Current MATLAB repo — eddy_uvdata.m (winding > 300°, closure < 10 km, Cahl 2023 clustering)

Red streamlines — 36 winding streamlines · White ellipse — fitted shape · ✕ — center 38.91°N, 75.04°W · isobaths from etopo1, GSHHS coastline.

What the current MATLAB repo upgrades

  • Cahl et al. 2023 clustering (inpolygon). The old method (Sadarjoen & Post 2000) merged two winding streamlines into one eddy when their centers were within a fixed distance (25 km) and rotated the same way — a tuning that depends on eddy size. The upgrade treats each streamline as a closed polygon and merges when one streamline's center lies inside the other's polygon (inpolygon containment test), or when the two polygons overlap in more than one point. Only if the polygons don't touch does it fall back to a center-distance test, now scaled to the radar resolution (2√2 × 2 km ≈ 5.7 km) instead of a fixed 25 km. Nested and concentric streamlines cluster correctly for any eddy size. Switchable via new_dist_thres (set 0 for the old method).
  • stream2_dc streamline integrator. Replaces MATLAB's stream2: velocity at each step is an inverse-distance-weighted average of the 4 surrounding grid nodes (NaN nodes skipped), stepping 0.2 of a cell up to 2000 vertices. No visited-cell bookkeeping, so circling streamlines keep winding — which is what lets the thresholds tighten to 300° winding / 10 km closure (from 270° / 25 km).
  • Consistent UTM zone handling — one zone is carried through the streamline, center, and ellipse conversions (geog2utm_nodisp / utm2ll).
  • Correct ellipse axes. Semi-axes are now √(2λ) of the covariance eigenvalues (the proper standard-deviational ellipse), not √λ.
  • Per-streamline output. Every clustered streamline's lat/lon is saved in the results .mat (save_streams), not just the summary numbers.
  • Publication-quality figure. Speed field, unit-vector quiver, labeled etopo1 bathymetry contours, GSHHS coastline patch, optional per-eddy zoom figures.
  • Pipeline conveniences. Local-data entry points (eddy_uvdata.m, eddy_uvdata_loop.m), parameter files (params_eddy1/2/3.m), tracking (eddy_tracking.m) and track analysis (analyze_eddy_tracks.m) stages, bundled sample data.

New: Python v2 — the MATLAB upgrades ported to Python

The eddy\v2 folder is a fresh Python port of the current MATLAB algorithm: stream2_dc.py (integrator), Cahl 2023 polygon clustering (matplotlib.path as inpolygon), exact ports of the UTM conversions, the √(2λ) ellipse, per-streamline saving, and a map figure with bathymetry and coastline read directly from the etopo1 binary. It ships with the same example data as the MATLAB repo (data/202004030900_…_NDBC.nc and data2.mat) and writes .mat results with identical variable names. Run with python eddy_uvdata.py.

Python v2 — eddy_uvdata.py (same parameters as MATLAB: 300°, 10 km, Cahl 2023)

1 eddy, 36 streamlines — identical to MATLAB. Land/coastline and labeled isobaths come from the same etopo1 file m_map uses; magenta streamlines with red centers, white fitted ellipse, ✕ eddy center.

The port is numerically faithful: every saved quantity agrees with MATLAB to at least 7 decimal places on this example.

Tracking pipeline on data2 — MATLAB vs Python v2

The full three-stage pipeline (identify every timestep → link into tracks → analyze tracks) run by both implementations on the repo's multi-timestep example data2: a 90×95 grid of 3 km WERA HF-radar currents in Long Bay (Carolinas shelf), 101 half-hour timesteps (~2 days). Python reads the data from data2.nc (the .mat converted to NetCDF; results are also convertible to .nc with ragged streamline storage via convert_data.py).

MATLAB — analyze_eddy_tracks.m

Tracks ≥ 12 timesteps. Blue = CCW (cyclonic), red = CW (anticyclonic); green ○ start, ✕ end.

Python v2 — analyze_eddy_tracks.py

Same three tracks, same title stats — the two cyclonic eddies drift northeast along the shelf-edge front.

Timestep 54 (2013-01-04 04:30 UTC) — 4 eddies

119, 21, 9, and 2 streamlines — eddies with fewer than 6 streamlines are kept in the results but not drawn.

Timestep 56 (2013-01-04 05:30 UTC) — 3 eddies

91, 55, and 5 streamlines — the main frontal eddy and the mid-shelf rotation during the multi-eddy period.

QuantityMATLAB repoPython v2 port
Eddies identified (101 timesteps)191191
Max eddies in one timestep55
Max streamlines in one eddy162162
Tracks1717
Track lengths (timesteps)90, 44, 33, 5, 4, 3, 2, and ten 1s — identical
Longest track90 timesteps (45 h), ~0.3 rotations/day — identical
Track center seriesall 17 tracks agree to 10⁻¹³ degrees (float64 rounding)
Identification wall time~36 min (single core, ~10–27 s/step)27.9 s (numba, 14 cores) — 24 min pure Python

The only detected difference is in the angular-velocity diagnostic (ω): MATLAB's scatteredInterpolant extrapolates outside the data hull while scipy's griddata returns NaN, giving 6 extra NaN ω values in Python and ≤ 10⁻³ deg/s differences elsewhere. Eddy detection, positions, shapes, and tracking are unaffected.

Performance: compiling the hot loops

The Python port's cost is concentrated in two scalar float64 loops per start point: the stream2_dc streamline integration (up to 2,000 Euler steps) and the winding-angle scan. Rust, C/MEX, and Cython were considered, but no compiler toolchain exists on this machine (no MSVC, gcc, or cargo) — while numba 0.66 installs cleanly on Python 3.14 and compiles through LLVM, the same code generator Rust uses, so for loops like these a Rust rewrite would deliver essentially the same machine code. Both kernels (kernels_numba.py) run with strict IEEE semantics (no fastmath) and produce bit-identical results to the pure-Python fallbacks, which remain in place when numba is absent.

Profiling then exposed a second, hidden bottleneck: the angular-velocity diagnostic re-triangulated each eddy streamline's search window (Delaunay + barycentric transform, ~200 ms each — ~42 s on a timestep with 206 winding streamlines). With fast_omega (default), ω is interpolated on one whole-grid triangulation per timestep instead. A/B tested on 6 timesteps: detection geometry (eddy counts, centers, directions, streamline coordinates, ellipse fits) is bit-identical either way; only ω shifts — up to ~10% relative on individual eddies but ≲ 10⁻⁴ deg/s absolute, smaller than the existing MATLAB↔Python interpolant difference. Set params['fast_omega']=0 for the exact per-window ω (~50 s/timestep).

data2 identification, one timestep (single core)Timevs MATLAB
MATLAB R2024b (JIT)~10–27 s1×
Python v2, pure Python~395 s~20× slower
+ numba kernels (bit-identical)~52 s~2.5× slower
+ fast_omega (ω diagnostic only)~1.5 s~15× faster
End-to-end (fresh timed run, NetCDF-native)MATLAB (single core)Python (+numba +fast_omega + 14 cores)
data2: identify 101 timesteps~36 min (single core)27.9 s (14 cores)
data2: trackingseconds2.4 s
data2: track analysis + figuresseconds1.3 s
data2 pipeline total~36 min31.7 s
Delaware Bay single-file example (incl. figure)~1 min~3 s

The Python pipeline is now fully NetCDF-native (no .mat files read or written; convert_data.py imports MATLAB data, verify_vs_matlab.py compares runs). Verified across all 101 timesteps after the final rerun: 191 eddies with zero count mismatches, centers within 10⁻¹³ degrees, ellipse angles within 4×10⁻¹¹ degrees, identical tracks — machine-precision agreement with MATLAB.

Side by side: v1.0 Python and v1.0 MATLAB on the same data

v1.0 Python — eddy1_identification.py core (winding > 270°, closure < 25 km)

16 winding streamlines, anticyclonic, center 38.89°N, 75.03°W. Run via run_local.py, which executes the v1.0 algorithm verbatim on the local file.

v1.0 MATLAB — eddy_uvdata.m (winding > 270°)

28 winding streamlines (magenta), blue fitted ellipse, same center. Older plotting style, no speed field or bathymetry.

*v1.0 MATLAB fits the ellipse in lon/lat degrees (0.018° × 0.040°), converted here at 39°N for comparison.

What the tests showed

All implementations agree on the eddy's existence, location, rotation sense, and orientation; the Python v2 port reproduces the MATLAB repo exactly.

Streamline counts differ between generations by design. The v1.0 Python integrates streamlines with a modified matplotlib streamplot, which terminates a trajectory when it re-enters an already-visited cell — circling paths get cut short. With the strict thresholds (300°, 10 km) the v1.0 core found zero eddies; its lenient defaults (270°, 25 km) compensate. The stream2_dc integrator (MATLAB repo and Python v2) removes that limitation.

The v1.0 live pipeline is currently blocked: master1.py hangs contacting the HFRnet THREDDS server, so the download-and-loop mode couldn't be tested end to end. The algorithm itself runs fine on local data.

Fixes needed for modern Python (3.14 / numpy 2.4 / matplotlib 3.10): removed np.warnings line in master1.py; dtype=np.float → float in eddy3; replaced removed cbook._check_in_list in streamplot_dc.py; installed netCDF4 and utm. Patched copies live in eddy\v1_py_test — originals untouched.
